critic_raw: The design uses 'patch-level embeddings aggregated by mean pooling or attention-based aggregation' for whole-slide image representation. Mean pooling loses spatial structure — for histopathology, the spatial arrangement of cell types is diagnostically relevant. Attention-based aggregation is more appropriate but is a more complex choice. The design says 'the choice is fixed before training and applied consistently' — but if mean pooling is chosen over attention aggregation, the model may be systematically disadvantaged for rare subtypes where spatial patterns matter.
defender_raw: The design presents both mean pooling and attention-based aggregation as valid options, with the choice fixed before training. The 'fix before training' requirement ensures no data-driven selection bias. If attention aggregation is more appropriate for the clinical task, the experimenter can make this choice based on domain knowledge. The primary comparison (multimodal vs. image-only) is valid regardless of which aggregation is used — both multimodal and image-only baselines would use the same aggregation strategy. If mean pooling is chosen and the model is disadvantaged for rare subtypes, this would appear in per-class F1 results, directing future work to attention aggregation. The design is honest about this choice.
adjudication_raw: One concern: mean pooling loses spatial structure important for rare subtype classification. Addressed: both models use the same aggregation strategy; any limitation would be visible in per-class F1; attention aggregation is available as the better choice. Verdict: defense_wins.
